differential.evidence_basis: Widespread dyspigmented (pale and light-brown) macules coalescing on the upper trunk of a young adult, flaring after a hot humid tropical holiday with sweating, and pale lesions that fail to tan — the classic Malassezia overgrowth pattern.
Also causes pale/depigmented patches, but is typically smooth and completely scale-free and favours acral/periorificial sites rather than the seborrhoeic upper trunk.
Truncal scaly eruption, but usually acute/self-limiting with a herald patch, collarette scale and a 'Christmas-tree' distribution rather than chronic dyspigmentation.
A great mimic that can produce a truncal papulosquamous rash; must be screened out by the absence of palmar/plantar involvement and generalised lymphadenopathy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening description the benign Malassezia pattern should lead, but a competent responder keeps vitiligo and — briefly — secondary syphilis on the list until screened, without alarming the patient.

differential.evidence_basis: Dyspigmentation + heat/sweat trigger + failure to tan + (in person) fine branny scale on stretch — a coherent benign picture.
No palmar/plantar rash and no lymphadenopathy on targeted screening (DI-005) — makes secondary syphilis very unlikely.

Cannot be fully excluded remotely because the confirmatory sign (fine scale on stretch) needs an in-person examination — hence routine GP confirmation is part of the correct plan, not an urgent escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The targeted mimic screen down-weights secondary syphilis. Vitiligo remains the honest remote-uncertainty, resolved by a routine clinician look — NOT by urgent referral.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign, non-contagious superficial yeast overgrowth; presumptive on telehealth from history + video appearance, confirmable in person by fine branny scale / Wood's lamp / KOH microscopy. Managed with topical antifungal and reassurance; the dyspigmentation persists for months after cure.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct endpoint is a confident benign presumptive diagnosis with topical antifungal advice, an explicit expectation that pale patches persist for months, and routine (non-urgent) GP confirmation to exclude vitiligo — with a secondary-infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of the affected skin — new blistering, hot/painful spreading redness, weeping or crusting
red_flags.clinical_significance: A benign superficial fungal rash does not blister or become hot and painful; these signs would indicate secondary bacterial infection/cellulitis needing prompt in-person antibiotic assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated spreading soft-tissue infection could progress to cellulitis or systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Systemic features — fever or feeling unwell — accompanying the skin changes
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pityriasis versicolor is a purely local, non-febrile condition; fever points away from it toward infection or a systemic process and warrants medical review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A systemic infection or alternative diagnosis could be mislabelled as a harmless rash.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting a systemic mimic (secondary syphilis) — palmar/plantar rash or generalised lymphadenopathy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secondary syphilis can mimic a truncal papulosquamous eruption; palmar/plantar involvement and lymphadenopathy are the discriminating features and were screened negative.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A treatable systemic infection could be missed if reassurance were given without the mimic screen.

pathophysiology_summary: Pityriasis (tinea) versicolor is caused by overgrowth of the commensal lipophilic yeast Malassezia (furfur/globosa) in its hyphal form within the stratum corneum. Heat, humidity, sweating and sebum production (hence the flare after a tropical holiday) promote the yeast-to-mycelial conversion. The organism produces azelaic and other dicarboxylic acids that inhibit tyrosinase in melanocytes, which is why affected areas fail to tan and appear hypopigmented against surrounding tanned skin; in other patients lesions appear hyperpigmented or mildly erythematous, giving the 'versicolor' (varied colour) appearance. Involvement of the seborrhoeic upper trunk, neck and proximal arms reflects the yeast's dependence on sebaceous lipids. The infection is superficial, benign and non-contagious. Fine 'branny' scale is released on stretching/scraping the surface (a bedside sign). Antifungal treatment kills the yeast within days, but the pigmentary change resolves only over weeks-to-months as the epidermis turns over and melanocyte function recovers — a key expectation-setting point, because patients often mistake the persisting pale marks for treatment failure.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Letting the patient's intense psychosocial distress, the 'disfiguring/look diseased' language and the out-of-hours framing escalate a benign, self-treatable condition to urgent in-person or ED review. The correct response de-escalates with reassurance and topical treatment.
Reinforcing the patient's fear that this is a spreading or catching disease. It is a non-contagious overgrowth of the patient's own commensal yeast, and saying so is therapeutic.

Reaching for oral terbinafine, which is effective for dermatophyte tinea but NOT for Malassezia versicolor — a classic error. Treatment is topical azole/selenium sulfide, or an oral azole (itraconazole/fluconazole) only for extensive/recurrent disease.
Treating a scaly 'rash' with a topical corticosteroid, which does not treat the yeast and can worsen or prolong it.

Giving reassurance without screening palms/soles and lymph nodes for the secondary-syphilis mimic, or dismissing vitiligo — the remote uncertainty that justifies a routine (not urgent) clinician look.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fine branny (dust-like) scale elicited by stretching or lightly scraping the lesions (Besnier's sign) — the key bedside confirmatory finding, which requires the clinician's hands
Wood's lamp examination for the characteristic yellow-green/coppery fluorescence
Skin-scraping KOH microscopy showing the 'spaghetti and meatballs' hyphae and spores
Definite exclusion of vitiligo (smooth, scale-free) versus a scaly dyspigmentation on palpation
Palpation for lymphadenopathy as part of the secondary-syphilis mimic screen
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No urgent escalation. Recognise the benign Malassezia pattern from history plus video appearance, reassure it is a harmless non-contagious yeast overgrowth, recommend a topical antifungal (ketoconazole 2% or selenium sulfide shampoo used as a body wash), set the expectation that pale marks persist for months after cure, and arrange routine GP review to confirm and exclude mimics. Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection (blistering / hot painful spreading skin / fever).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for benign management (topical antifungal is safe and appropriate even presumptively). The real risks are (1) OVER-action — escalating a benign rash to urgent in-person/ED under the pressure of the patient's distress; and (2) reassurance without the mimic screen or without routine confirmation, given vitiligo cannot be excluded remotely.
